en.content: For a constant hydraulic stress on an object, the fractional change in the object's volume $$(\Delta V / V)$$ and its bulk modulus $$B$$ are related as
en.explanation:
To understand the relationship between the fractional change in volume of an object $$(\Delta V / V)$$ and its bulk modulus $$(B)$$ under a constant hydraulic stress, we need to recall the definition and formula of bulk modulus.

The bulk modulus $$(B)$$ is defined as the ratio of the hydraulic stress (pressure change) to the corresponding relative change in volume (fractional change in volume). Mathematically, it can be expressed as:

$$B = -\frac{\Delta P}{\frac{\Delta V}{V}}$$

where:

  • $$B$$ is the bulk modulus
  • $$(\Delta P)$$ is the change in pressure (hydraulic stress)
  • $$(\Delta V / V)$$ is the fractional change in volume

From the above equation, we can solve for the fractional change in volume $$(\Delta V / V)$$:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = -\frac{\Delta P}{B}$$

Here, it becomes clear that the fractional change in volume $$(\Delta V / V)$$ is inversely proportional to the bulk modulus $$(B)$$, assuming the change in pressure $$(\Delta P)$$ remains constant. This means that if the bulk modulus increases, the fractional change in volume decreases, and vice versa.

Thus, the correct option is:

Option C

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} \propto \frac{1}{B}$$

en.content: A common emitter amplifier has a voltage gain of 50 an input impedance of 100 $$\Omega$$ and an output impedance of 400 $$\Omega$$. The power gain of the amplifier is
en.explanation:
To find the power gain of the amplifier, we first need to understand the relationship between voltage gain, input impedance, and output impedance.

The power gain (GP) of an amplifier is given by the product of the voltage gain (GV) and the current gain (GI). However, we can derive it directly from the voltage gain and the impedance values.

Given:

Voltage gain (GV) = 50

Input impedance (Rin) = 100 $$\Omega$$

Output impedance (Rout) = 400 $$\Omega$$

We know that:

$$ G_P = \frac{V_{out}^2 / R_{out}}{V_{in}^2 / R_{in}} $$

Since the voltage gain is:

$$ G_V = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 50 $$

Substituting $$ G_V = 50 $$ into the power gain formula:

$$ G_P = \left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_{out}} = G_V^2 \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_{out}} $$

So we have:

$$ G_P = 50^2 \cdot \frac{100}{400} $$

$$ G_P = 2500 \cdot 0.25 $$

$$ G_P = 625 $$

Hence, the power gain of the amplifier is 625.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option C - 625

en.content: Two rings of radius $$R$$ and $$n R$$ made of same material have the ratio of moment of inertia about an axis passing through centre is $$1: 64$$. The value of $$n$$ is
en.explanation:
To determine the value of $$n$$, let's analyze the problem by considering the moment of inertia for each ring about an axis passing through its center. For a ring, the moment of inertia about an axis through its center can be expressed as:

$$I = mR^2$$

Where:

  • $$I$$ is the moment of inertia
  • $$m$$ is the mass of the ring
  • $$R$$ is the radius of the ring

Given that both rings are made of the same material, they will have the same density. Let's denote the moment of inertia of the first ring (radius $$R$$) as $$I_1$$ and the moment of inertia of the second ring (radius $$nR$$) as $$I_2$$. According to the problem:

$$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{1}{64}$$

For the first ring (radius $$R$$):

$$I_1 = m_1 R^2$$

For the second ring (radius $$nR$$):

$$I_2 = m_2 (nR)^2 = m_2 n^2 R^2$$

Assuming both rings are made of the same material and have the same mass per unit length, the mass of the second ring with radius $$nR$$ will be $$n$$ times the mass of the first ring (since circumference scales linearly with radius):

$$m_2 = n m_1$$

Then we can write:

$$I_2 = n m_1 (nR)^2 = n m_1 n^2 R^2 = n^3 m_1 R^2$$

Using the ratio of the moments of inertia:

$$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{m_1 R^2}{n^3 m_1 R^2} = \frac{1}{n^3} = \frac{1}{64}$$

The above equality simplifies to:

$$n^3 = 64$$

$$n = \sqrt[3]{64} = 4$$

Thus, the value of $$n$$ is:

Option C

en.content: In an electron gun the potential difference between the filament and plate is $$4000 \mathrm{~V}$$. What will be the velocity of electron emitting from the gain?
en.explanation:
To determine the velocity of the electron emitted from the gun, we need to apply the concept of energy conservation. Specifically, the kinetic energy (KE) gained by the electron will be equal to the electrostatic potential energy (PE) provided by the potential difference (V) between the filament and the plate.

The electrostatic potential energy can be expressed as:

$$ PE = eV $$

where:

  • $$ e $$ is the charge of the electron, approximately $$ 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C} $$
  • $$ V $$ is the potential difference, which is given as $$ 4000 \text{ V} $$

The kinetic energy of the electron can be expressed as:

$$ KE = \frac{1}{2} mv^2 $$

where:

  • $$ m $$ is the mass of the electron, approximately $$ 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg} $$
  • $$ v $$ is the velocity of the electron we need to determine.

Equating the kinetic energy to the potential energy, we have:

$$ eV = \frac{1}{2} mv^2 $$

Rearranging the equation to solve for the velocity $$ v $$ gives:

$$ v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}} $$

Substituting the given values:

$$ v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4000}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}} $$

$$ v = \sqrt{\frac{1.28 \times 10^{-15}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}} $$

$$ v = \sqrt{1.41 \times 10^{15}} $$

$$ v \approx 3.75 \times 10^7 \text{ m/s} $$

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option D: $$ 3.75 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

en.content: The fundamental frequency of an open organ pipe is $$600 \mathrm{~Hz}$$. The first overtone of this pipe has same frequency as first overtone of a closed organ pipe. If speed of sound is $$330 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$, then the length of a closed organ pipe is
en.explanation:
To solve this problem, we need to understand the relationship between the frequencies of open and closed organ pipes and how their lengths are connected to these frequencies.

Firstly, let’s consider the open organ pipe. The fundamental frequency of an open organ pipe is given by:

$$ f_1 = \frac{v}{2L_1} $$

where $ f_1 = 600 \, \text{Hz} $ is the fundamental frequency, $ v = 330 \, \text{m/s} $ is the speed of sound, and $ L_1 $ is the length of the open organ pipe.

Hence:

$$ 600 = \frac{330}{2L_1} $$

Solving for $ L_1 $, we get:

$$ L_1 = \frac{330}{2 \times 600} = \frac{330}{1200} = 0.275 \, \text{m} = 27.5 \, \text{cm} $$

For the open organ pipe, the first overtone frequency will be:

$$ f_2 = \frac{2v}{2L_1} = \frac{v}{L_1} = \frac{330}{0.275} \, \text{m} = 1200 \, \text{Hz} $$

Now, considering the closed organ pipe, the first overtone frequency for the closed organ pipe (which corresponds to the third harmonic) is given by:

$$ f'_2 = \frac{3v}{4L_2} $$

We are given that the first overtone of the open organ pipe (which is $ 1200 \, \text{Hz} $) is equal to the first overtone of the closed organ pipe. Hence:

$$ 1200 = \frac{3 \times 330}{4L_2} $$

Solving for $ L_2 $, we get:

$$ 1200 = \frac{990}{4L_2} $$

Rearranging:

$$ 4L_2 = \frac{990}{1200} $$

$$ L_2 = \frac{990}{4800} \, \text{m} = 0.20625 \, \text{m} = 20.625 \, \text{cm} $$

Given the options, there appears to be a minor discrepancy. However, the closest value is:

Option A: 21 cm

en.content: Magnetic moment of bar magnet is $$2 M$$. The work done to turn the magnet by $$90^{\circ}$$ of magnet in direction of magnetic field $$B / 2$$ will be
en.explanation:
To find the work done in turning the magnet, we need to understand the relationship between the magnetic moment, the magnetic field, and the potential energy in the system. The potential energy (U) of a magnetic dipole in a magnetic field is given by the formula:

$$U = -\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}$$

where:

$$\vec{M}$$ = Magnetic moment vector

$$\vec{B}$$ = Magnetic field vector

For a bar magnet with a magnetic moment $$2M$$ in a magnetic field $$\frac{B}{2}$$, the potential energy can be simplified as:

$$U = - (2M) \left( \frac{B}{2} \right) \cos(\theta)$$

Initially, let's consider when the magnet is aligned with the magnetic field:

$$\theta = 0^\circ$$

$$U_1 = - (2M) \left( \frac{B}{2} \right) \cos(0^\circ)$$

$$U_1 = - (2M) \left( \frac{B}{2} \right) \cdot 1$$

$$U_1 = -MB$$

Next, let's consider the state when the magnet is turned by $$90^\circ$$:

$$\theta = 90^\circ$$

$$U_2 = - (2M) \left( \frac{B}{2} \right) \cos(90^\circ)$$

$$U_2 = - (2M) \left( \frac{B}{2} \right) \cdot 0$$

$$U_2 = 0$$

The work done (W) is equal to the change in potential energy, which is:

$$W = U_1 - U_2$$

$$W = (-MB) - 0$$

$$W = MB$$

Hence, the work done to turn the magnet by $$90^\circ$$ in a magnetic field $$\frac{B}{2}$$ is:

Option D: $$MB$$

en.content: A resistance $$R$$ and inductance $$L$$ and a capacitor $$C$$ all are connected in series with an $$\mathrm{AC}$$ supply. The resistance of $$R$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$ and for a given frequency, the inductive reactance of $$L$$ is 36 ohm and capacitive reactance of $$C$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$. If the current in the circuit is $$5 \mathrm{~amp}$$. Find the potential difference across $$R, L$$ and $$C$$.
en.explanation:
To determine the potential differences across the resistor $$R$$, inductor $$L$$, and capacitor $$C$$ in a series AC circuit, we can use Ohm's Law and the given impedances.

Given:

  • $$R = 24 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Inductive reactance $$X_L = 36 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Capacitive reactance $$X_C = 24 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Current in the circuit $$I = 5 \textrm{ amp}$$

First, we find the potential difference across each component using Ohm's Law:

  1. Potential difference across $$R$$:

$$ V_R = I \cdot R $$

$$ V_R = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_R = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$L$$:

$$ V_L = I \cdot X_L $$

$$ V_L = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 36 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_L = 180 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$C$$:

$$ V_C = I \cdot X_C $$

$$ V_C = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_C = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

Summarizing, the potential differences are:

  • $$V_R = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_L = 180 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_C = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: $$120, 180, 120 \textrm{ V}$$

en.content: The potential energy for a force field $$\mathbf{F}$$ is given by $$u(x, y)=\cos (x+y)$$. The force acting on a particle at position given by co-ordinates $$(0, \pi / 6)$$ is
en.explanation:
To find the force acting on a particle in a force field given by a potential energy function, we can use the relationship between force and potential energy. The force is the negative gradient of the potential energy function:

$$\mathbf{F} = -\nabla u(x, y)$$

Given the potential energy function:

$$u(x, y) = \cos(x + y)$$

The gradient operator $$\nabla$$ in two dimensions is given by:

$$\nabla = \left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\right)$$

To find the force, we first need to compute the partial derivatives of $$u(x, y)$$ with respect to $$x$$ and $$y$$.

Calculate the partial derivative of $$u(x, y)$$ with respect to $$x$$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\cos(x + y) = -\sin(x + y)$$

Now, calculate the partial derivative of $$u(x, y)$$ with respect to $$y$$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\cos(x + y) = -\sin(x + y)$$

So the gradient of $$u(x, y)$$ is:

$$\nabla u(x, y) = \left(-\sin(x + y), -\sin(x + y)\right)$$

The force $$\mathbf{F}$$ is the negative of this gradient:

$$\mathbf{F} = -\nabla u(x, y) = \left(\sin(x + y), \sin(x + y)\right)$$

We need to evaluate this at the coordinates $$(0, \pi / 6)$$:

$$ (x, y) = (0, \pi / 6) $$

Substitute these values into the sine function:

$$ \sin(0 + \pi / 6) = \sin(\pi / 6) = \frac{1}{2} $$

Thus, the force $$\mathbf{F}$$ at $$(0, \pi / 6)$$ is:

$$\mathbf{F} = \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

In vector notation, this can be written as:

$$ \mathbf{F} = \frac{1}{2} \hat{\mathbf{i}} + \frac{1}{2} \hat{\mathbf{j}} $$

This matches with Option B:

$$\frac{1}{2}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}})$$

en.content: A Carnot's heat engine works between the temperature $$527^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ and $$127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$. What amount of heat should it consume per second to deliver mechanical work at the rate of $$1.0 \mathrm{~kW}$$ ?
en.explanation:
A Carnot heat engine operates between two temperatures: the hot reservoir temperature ($ T_h $) and the cold reservoir temperature ($ T_c $). To find the efficiency ($ \eta $) of a Carnot engine, we use the formula:

$$ \eta = 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h} $$

where both temperatures ($ T_h $ and $ T_c $) must be in absolute temperature, i.e., Kelvin (K).

The given temperatures are $ 527^{\circ} \mathrm{C} $ and $ 127^{\circ} \mathrm{C} $. First, we convert these temperatures to Kelvin:

$$ T_h = 527^{\circ} \mathrm{C} + 273 = 800 \, \mathrm{K} $$

$$ T_c = 127^{\circ} \mathrm{C} + 273 = 400 \, \mathrm{K} $$

Next, we calculate the efficiency of the Carnot engine:

$$ \eta = 1 - \frac{400}{800} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 $$

The efficiency ($ \eta $) is equal to the fraction of heat converted to work. Therefore, the fraction of heat used to do mechanical work per second is:

$$ \eta = \frac{W}{Q_h} $$

where $ W $ is the work done per second (which is $ 1.0 \, \text{kW} $ or $ 1000 \, \text{W} $), and $ Q_h $ is the heat consumed per second.

Rearranging the formula, we get:

$$ Q_h = \frac{W}{\eta} $$

Substituting the given values:

$$ Q_h = \frac{1000 \, \mathrm{W}}{0.5} = 2000 \, \mathrm{W} $$

To convert the heat consumed per second from Watts to kcal/s, we use the conversion factor $ 1 \, \mathrm{kcal/s} = 4184 \, \mathrm{W} $:

$$ Q_h = \frac{2000 \, \mathrm{W}}{4184 \, \mathrm{W/kcal}} \approx 0.477 \, \mathrm{kcal/s} $$

Therefore, the amount of heat the Carnot engine should consume per second to deliver mechanical work at the rate of $ 1.0 \, \text{kW} $ is:

Option A: 0.477 kcal/s

If the temperature of the room is $$20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ then what will be the number of beats produced per second by these waves. When the speed of sound at $$0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ is $$332 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$ ?
en.explanation:
Beats will be formed as the wavelengths (hence frequencies) are similar.

$$\begin{aligned} & \lambda_1=40 \mathrm{~cm} ~\lambda_2=42 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & v \propto \sqrt{T} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{273+20}{273}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad v_2=319.23 \\ & v_1=\frac{v_1}{\lambda_1}=\frac{319.23}{0.40}=798.075 \mathrm{~Hz} \\ & v_2=\frac{v_2}{\lambda_2}=\frac{319.23}{0.42}=760.071 \mathrm{~Hz} . \end{aligned}$$

No. of beats $$=v_2-v_1=798-760=38 \mathrm{~Hz}$$

en.content: Water is flowing on a horizontal fixed surface such that its flow velocity varies with $$y$$ (vertical direction) as $$v=k\left(\frac{2 y^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^3}{a^3}\right)$$. If coefficient of viscosity for water is $$2 \eta$$. What will the shear stress between layers of water at $$y=a$$ ?
en.explanation:
To determine the shear stress between layers of water at $$y=a$$, we start by using the given velocity profile and the definition of shear stress for a fluid in laminar flow.

The shear stress $$\tau$$ in a fluid is given by:

$$ \tau = \eta \frac{du}{dy} $$

where $$\eta$$ is the dynamic viscosity of the fluid, and $$\frac{du}{dy}$$ is the velocity gradient in the direction perpendicular to the flow.

Given the velocity profile:

$$ v = k \left( \frac{2 y^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^3}{a^3} \right) $$

First, we need to calculate the velocity gradient $$\frac{dv}{dy}$$:

$$ \frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy} \left( k \left( \frac{2 y^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^3}{a^3} \right) \right) $$

Applying the differentiation, we get:

$$ \frac{dv}{dy} = k \left( \frac{d}{dy} \left( \frac{2 y^2}{a^2} \right) - \frac{d}{dy} \left( \frac{y^3}{a^3} \right) \right) $$

Differentiating term by term, we have:

$$ \frac{dv}{dy} = k \left( \frac{2 \cdot 2y}{a^2} - \frac{3y^2}{a^3} \right) = k \left( \frac{4y}{a^2} - \frac{3y^2}{a^3} \right) $$

We need to evaluate this gradient at $$y = a$$:

$$ \left. \frac{dv}{dy} \right|_{y=a} = k \left( \frac{4a}{a^2} - \frac{3a^2}{a^3} \right) = k \left( \frac{4a}{a^2} - \frac{3a^2}{a^3} \right) = k \left( \frac{4}{a} - \frac{3}{a} \right) = k \left( \frac{4-3}{a} \right) = \frac{k}{a} $$

Now, using the given viscosity of the water, $$2 \eta$$, the shear stress $$\tau$$ is given by:

$$ \tau = 2 \eta \left( \frac{k}{a} \right) = \frac{2 \eta k}{a} $$

Therefore, the shear stress between layers of water at $$y=a$$ is:

$$ \tau = \frac{2 \eta k}{a} $$

The correct option is Option A:

$$ \frac{2 \eta k}{a} $$

en.content: An electron of mass $$m$$ and charge $$e$$ initially at rest gets accelerated by a constant field $$2 E$$. The rate of change of de-Broglie wavelength of this electron at time $$t$$ ignoring relativistic effects is
en.explanation:
To determine the rate of change of the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron at time $$t$$, we need to start with the expression for the de-Broglie wavelength, which is given by

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

where $$h$$ is Planck's constant and $$p$$ is the momentum of the electron.

Since the electron experiences a constant electric field of $$2E$$, it undergoes a uniform acceleration. The force $ F $ on the electron is given by

$$F = e \cdot 2E = 2eE$$

This force causes the electron to accelerate, so the acceleration $a$ is given by

$$a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{2eE}{m}$$

Initially, the electron is at rest, so its velocity $v$ at time $t$ can be obtained using the equation of motion:

$$v = u + at = 0 + \left(\frac{2eE}{m}\right) t = \frac{2eE t}{m}$$

The momentum $$p$$ of the electron at time $$t$$ is given by

$$p = mv = m \left( \frac{2eE t}{m} \right) = 2eE t$$

Now substituting the expression for momentum into the de-Broglie wavelength equation, we get

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{2eE t}$$

To find the rate of change of de-Broglie wavelength with respect to time, we take the derivative of $$\lambda$$ with respect to $$t$$:

$$\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \left( \frac{h}{2eE t} \right) = h \cdot \frac{d}{dt} \left( \frac{1}{2eE t} \right) = h \cdot \left( \frac{-1}{(2eE t)^2} \cdot 2eE \right) = \frac{-h}{2eE} \cdot \frac{1}{t^2} \cdot 2eE = \frac{-h}{t^2}$$

Thus, the rate of change of de-Broglie wavelength of the electron at time $$t$$ is

$$\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = \frac{-h}{2eE t^2}$$

So the correct answer is:

Option A: $$-\frac{h}{\left(2eEt^2\right)}$$

en.content: A man of mass $m$ starts falling towards a planet of mass $$M$$ and radius $$R$$. As he reaches near to the surface, he realizes that will pass through a small role in the planet. As he enters the role, we sees that the planet is really made of two pieces a spherical shell of negligible thickness of mass $$3 M / 4$$ and a point mass $$M / 4$$ at the centre. Change in the force of gravity experienced by the man is
en.explanation:
When the man is outside the planet and close to its surface, the gravitational force is determined by the entire mass of the planet, $$M$$. The force of gravity at the surface can be given by Newton's law of gravitation:

$$ F = \frac{G M m}{R^2} $$

However, as the man passes through the hole in the planet, the gravitational force he experiences changes due to the unique mass distribution of the planet. The planet consists of a spherical shell of mass $$\frac{3M}{4}$$ and negligible thickness, and a point mass $$\frac{M}{4}$$ at the center.

Let's analyze the gravitational force when the man is inside the planet:

1. Spherical Shell Contribution:

A spherical shell of uniform density exerts no net gravitational force on a particle located inside it. Thus, the man inside the shell experiences no force from the spherical shell of mass $$\frac{3M}{4}$$.

2. Point Mass Contribution:

The point mass at the center, which is $$\frac{M}{4}$$, exerts a gravitational force on the man. According to Newton's law of gravitation, this force is given by:

$$ F' = \frac{G \left(\frac{M}{4}\right) m}{R^2} $$

To determine the change in the force of gravity experienced by the man, we subtract the force due to the point mass from the original force at the planet's surface:

Original force at the surface:

$$ F = \frac{G M m}{R^2} $$

Force inside due to point mass:

$$ F' = \frac{1}{4} \frac{G M m}{R^2} $$

Change in the force:

$$ \Delta F = F - F' $$

$$ \Delta F = \frac{G M m}{R^2} - \frac{1}{4} \frac{G M m}{R^2} $$

Simplifying the expression:

$$ \Delta F = \frac{G M m}{R^2} \left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) $$

$$ \Delta F = \frac{G M m}{R^2} \cdot \frac{3}{4} $$

$$ \Delta F = \frac{3}{4} \frac{G M m}{R^2} $$

Hence, the change in the force of gravity experienced by the man is

$$ \frac{3}{4} \frac{G M m}{R^2} $$

The correct answer is Option A: $$\frac{3}{4} \frac{G M m}{R^2}$$.

en.content: A vessel containing 1 mole of $$\mathrm{O}_2$$ gas (molar mass 32) at temperature $$T$$. The pressure of the gas is $$p$$. An identical vessel containing one mole of He gas (molar mass 4) at temperature $$4 T$$ has a pressure of
en.explanation:
To determine the pressure of the helium (He) gas in the identical vessel, we can use the ideal gas law:

$$PV = nRT$$

Given that both vessels are identical, they have the same volume, $$V$$. Also, each vessel contains one mole of gas, so $$n = 1$$ for both cases. Let's first express the pressure $$p$$ of the oxygen (O2) gas in terms of the given conditions:

For the oxygen gas:

$$pV = nRT$$

Substituting the given values:

$$pV = (1 \text{ mole}) \cdot R \cdot T$$

Therefore,

$$pV = RT$$

Now, consider the helium gas in the other vessel at temperature $$4T$$. According to the ideal gas law:

$$P_{He}V = nRT_{He}$$

Here, $${T_{He} = 4T}$$. Substituting the known values:

$$P_{He}V = (1 \text{ mole}) \cdot R \cdot (4T)$$

Therefore,

$$P_{He}V = 4RT$$

Since $$pV = RT$$ from our earlier equation for the oxygen gas, we can substitute $$RT$$ with $$pV$$ in the helium pressure equation:

$$P_{He}V = 4(pV)$$

Dividing both sides by $$V$$ to isolate $$P_{He}$$:

$$P_{He} = 4p$$

Therefore, the pressure of the helium gas at temperature $$4T$$ is $$4p$$.

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option C: 4p

en.content: When a string is divided into four segments of $$l_1, l_2, l_3$$ and $$l_4$$. The fundamental frequencies of these three segments are $$v_1, v_2, v_3$$ and $$v_4$$, respectively. The original fundamental frequency $$(v)$$ of the string is
en.explanation:
The fundamental frequency of a string is inversely proportional to its length. So, if the string is divided into four segments of lengths $$l_1, l_2, l_3$$ and $$l_4$$, then the fundamental frequencies of these segments will be inversely proportional to their lengths.

Let the original length of the string be $$L$$. Then, we have:

$$l_1 + l_2 + l_3 + l_4 = L$$

The fundamental frequency of the original string is given by:

$$v = \frac{1}{2L} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

where $$T$$ is the tension in the string and $$\mu$$ is the mass per unit length of the string.

The fundamental frequencies of the four segments are given by:

$$v_1 = \frac{1}{2l_1} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{1}{2l_2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

$$v_3 = \frac{1}{2l_3} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

$$v_4 = \frac{1}{2l_4} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

Adding these equations, we get:

$$v_1 + v_2 + v_3 + v_4 = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} \left(\frac{1}{l_1} + \frac{1}{l_2} + \frac{1}{l_3} + \frac{1}{l_4} \right)$$

Now, using the equation $$l_1 + l_2 + l_3 + l_4 = L$$, we can write:

$$\frac{1}{l_1} + \frac{1}{l_2} + \frac{1}{l_3} + \frac{1}{l_4} = \frac{L}{l_1 l_2 l_3 l_4}$$

Substituting this into the previous equation, we get:

$$v_1 + v_2 + v_3 + v_4 = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} \frac{L}{l_1 l_2 l_3 l_4}$$

Since $$v = \frac{1}{2L} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$, we can write:

$$v_1 + v_2 + v_3 + v_4 = \frac{vL}{l_1 l_2 l_3 l_4}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{v_1} + \frac{1}{v_2} + \frac{1}{v_3} + \frac{1}{v_4}}$$

This is option C.

en.content: After two hours one-eight of the starting amount of a certain radioactive isotope remained undecayed. The half-life of the isotope is
en.explanation:
To determine the half-life of the radioactive isotope, we need to use the information given and the formula for radioactive decay. The formula for radioactive decay is:

$$N(t) = N_0 \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{t}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

where:

  • $N(t)$ is the remaining quantity of the isotope at time $t$.
  • $N_0$ is the initial quantity of the isotope.
  • $T_{\frac{1}{2}}$ is the half-life of the isotope.
  • $t$ is the elapsed time.

According to the given information, after 2 hours (which is 120 minutes), one-eighth of the starting amount of the isotope remains undecayed. Therefore, if $\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = \frac{1}{8}$, we can write:

$$\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{t}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

Substituting the values we have:

$$\frac{1}{8} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{120}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

We know that $\frac{1}{8}$ can be written as $\left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^3$, therefore:

$$\left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^3 = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\frac{120}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}}$$

By equating the exponents, we get:

$$3 = \frac{120}{T_{\frac{1}{2}}}$$

Solving for $T_{\frac{1}{2}}$:

$$T_{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{120}{3} = 40 \ \text{minutes}$$

Thus, the half-life of the isotope is 40 minutes. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: 40 min

en.content: A direct current of $$10 \mathrm{~A}$$ is superimposed on an alternating current $$i=10 \sin \omega t$$ flowing through the wire. The effective value of the resulting current will be.
en.explanation:
The resulting current is the sum of the direct current and the alternating current:

$$I = I_{dc} + I_{ac} = 10 + 10 \sin \omega t$$

To find the effective value (also known as the root-mean-square or RMS value), we need to calculate the square root of the mean of the squared current:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} I^2 dt}$$

where T is the period of the alternating current.

Let's substitute the expression for I and simplify:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (10 + 10 \sin \omega t)^2 dt}$$

Expanding the square and integrating term by term:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (100 + 200 \sin \omega t + 100 \sin^2 \omega t) dt}$$

We know that the average value of $$ \sin \omega t $$ over a complete cycle is zero, and the average value of $$ \sin^2 \omega t $$ over a complete cycle is $$\frac{1}{2}$$. Therefore:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} (100T + 0 + 100 \cdot \frac{1}{2} T)} = \sqrt{150}$$

Simplifying further:

$$I_{rms} = 5 \sqrt{6} \mathrm{~A}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B: $$5 \sqrt{6} \mathrm{~A}$$.

en.content: A vessel is half filled with a liquid of refractive index $$\mu$$. The other half of the vessel is filled with an immiscible liquid of refractive index $$2 \mu$$. The apparent depth of the vessel is $$50 \%$$ of the actual depth. Then $$\mu$$ is
en.explanation:
Let's denote the actual depth of the vessel as $$d$$. Given that the vessel is half-filled with one liquid and the other half with the second liquid, each liquid fills a depth of $$\frac{d}{2}$$.

Let the refractive index of the first liquid be $$\mu$$ and the refractive index of the second liquid be $$2 \mu$$.

The formula to find the apparent depth due to a single layer of liquid is:

$$\text{Apparent Depth} = \frac{\text{Actual Depth}}{\text{Refractive Index}}$$

First, we find the apparent depth for each segment of the vessel:

For the layer with refractive index $$\mu$$:

$$\text{Apparent Depth}_1 = \frac{\frac{d}{2}}{\mu}$$

For the layer with refractive index $$2 \mu$$:

$$\text{Apparent Depth}_2 = \frac{\frac{d}{2}}{2 \mu}$$

The total apparent depth is the sum of the apparent depths of both layers:

$$\text{Total Apparent Depth} = \text{Apparent Depth}_1 + \text{Apparent Depth}_2$$

$$\text{Total Apparent Depth} = \frac{\frac{d}{2}}{\mu} + \frac{\frac{d}{2}}{2 \mu}$$

$$\text{Total Apparent Depth} = \frac{d}{2 \mu} + \frac{d}{4 \mu}$$

$$\text{Total Apparent Depth} = \frac{2d + d}{4 \mu}$$

$$\text{Total Apparent Depth} = \frac{3d}{4 \mu}$$

We are given that the apparent depth of the vessel is $$50 \%$$ of the actual depth. Therefore:

$$\frac{3d}{4 \mu} = \frac{d}{2}$$

We solve for $$\mu$$ by isolating it on one side of the equation. Multiply both sides by $$4 \mu$$:

$$3d = 2d \mu$$

Divide both sides by $$2d$$:

$$\mu = \frac{3}{2}$$

$$\mu = 1.5$$

So, the correct option is:

Option B: 1.5

en.content: In a YDSE, the light of wavelength $$\lambda=5000\mathop A\limits^o $$ is used, which emerges in phase from two slits at $$a$$ distance $$d=3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$$ apart. A transparent sheet of thickness $$t=1.5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$$ and refractive index $$\mu=1.25$$ is placed over one of the slits. What is the new angular position of the central maxima of the interference pattern, from the centre of the screen? Find the value of $$y$$.
en.explanation:
On introduction of a transparent sheet,

$$\Delta x=(\mu-1) t$$

if the central maxima occupies position of the nth fringe, then $$(\mu-1) t=n \lambda=d \sin \theta$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta & =\frac{(\mu-1)}{d} t \\ & =\frac{(1.25-1) \times 1.5 \times 10^{-7}}{3 \times 10^{-7}}=0.125 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, central maxima has angular position, $$\theta=\sin ^{-1}(0.125)=7.18^{\circ} \approx 7.2^{\circ}$$

for small angles, $$\sin \theta \approx \tan \theta$$

$$\begin{aligned} \tan \theta & =\frac{y}{D} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{y}{D} & =\frac{(\mu-1) t}{d} \Rightarrow y=\frac{D(\mu-1)}{d} t \end{aligned}$$

en.content: The masses of block $$A$$ and $$B$$ are $$m$$ and $$M$$, respectively. Between $$A$$ and $$B$$, there is a constant frictional force $$F$$ and $$B$$ can slide on a smooth horizontal surface. A is set in motion with velocity $$3 v_0$$ while $$B$$ is at rest. What is the distance moved by $$A$$ relative to $$B$$ before they move with the same velocity?
en.explanation:
Consider the FBD as given below.

Equations of motion,

$$\begin{aligned} & a_A=\frac{F}{m}(\text { in }-x \text { direction }) \\ & a_B=\frac{F}{M}(\text { in }+x \text { direction }) \end{aligned}$$

Thus, we have, relative accleration of $$A$$ w.r.t. $$B$$ as

$$\begin{aligned} a_{A B} & =a_A-a_B=\frac{-F}{m}-\frac{F}{M} \\ & =-F\left(\frac{M+m}{M m}\right) \quad \text{(along} ~\mathrm{-x}-\text{direction)} \end{aligned}$$

Initial relative velocities of $$A$$ w.r.t $$B, u_{A B}=3 v_0$$ using equation $$v^2-u^2+2 a s$$,

$$\begin{aligned} 0 & =9 v_0^2-\frac{2 F(M+m)}{M m} s \\ \Rightarrow \quad s & =\frac{9 M m v_0^2}{2 F(M+m)} \end{aligned}$$

Distance moved relative to $$B, S_{A B}=\frac{9 M m v_0^2}{2 F(M+m)}$$

en.content: A thin plano-convex lens of focal length $$f$$ is split into two halves one of the halves is shifted along the optical axis. The separation between object and image formed by one of the half-lenses is 2.4 and the magnification of same lens is 2. If $$f$$ and $$d$$ be the focal length of the lens and separation between the two halves respectively then
en.explanation:
Using lens formula,

Given, $$u_2+v_2=2.4 \mathrm{~m} \quad \text{.... (i)}$$

Also, magnification for lens $$(1)$$ is 2.

$$2=\frac{v_2+d}{u_2-d} \quad \text{... (ii)}$$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii),

$$u_2=0.8+d, v_2=1.6-d$$

From lens formula,

$$\frac{1}{v_2+d}+\frac{1}{u_2-d}=\frac{1}{f}$$ (For lens (1))

$$\frac{1}{v_2}+\frac{1}{u_2}=\frac{1}{f}$$ (For lens (2))

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{v_2+d}+\frac{1}{u_2-d}=\frac{1}{v_2}+\frac{1}{u_2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{1.6-d+d}+\frac{1}{0.8+d-d}=\frac{1}{1.6-d}+\frac{1}{0.8+d} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{1.6}+\frac{1}{0.8}=\frac{(0.8+d)+(1.6-d)}{(1.6-d)(0.8+d)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1+2}{1.6}=\frac{24}{(1.6-\mathrm{d})(0.8-d)} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{3}{1.6}=\frac{2.4}{(1.6-d)(0.8-d)} \\ \Rightarrow \quad-d^2+0.8 d+1.28=1.28 \\ d^2-0.8 d=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & d(d-0.8)=0 \\ \therefore \quad & d=0.8 \mathrm{~m} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{1.6-0.8}+\frac{1}{0.8+0.8}=\frac{1}{f} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{1}{0.53} \text { or } f=0.53 \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} x & =A \sin \omega t=1 \sin \frac{2 \pi}{T} \cdot t=\sin \frac{2 \pi}{8} t \\ \therefore \quad x & =\sin \left(\frac{\pi t}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Velocity, $$v=\frac{d x}{d t}$$

$$=\frac{d}{d t} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) t=\frac{\pi}{4} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) t$$

$$\therefore$$ Acceleration of the particle.

$$\begin{aligned} a & =\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{\pi}{4} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) t\right] \\ & =-\frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t\right) \Rightarrow a=-\frac{\pi^2}{16} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} \cdot t\right) \end{aligned}$$

At $$t=2 \mathrm{~s}$$

$$a=-\frac{\pi^2}{16} \cdot \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 2\right)=-\frac{\pi^2}{16} \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$$

en.content: $$10 \mathrm{~g}$$ each of $$\mathrm{CH}_4$$ and $$\mathrm{O}_2$$ are kept in cylinders of same volume under same temperature, give the pressure ratio of two gases
en.explanation:
To determine the pressure ratio of the two gases, we can use the ideal gas law given by

$$PV = nRT$$

where:

  • $ P $ = Pressure
  • $ V $ = Volume
  • $ n $ = Number of moles
  • $ R $ = Universal gas constant
  • $ T $ = Temperature

Since the volumes and temperatures of both gases are the same, we can simplify the relationship to

$$P \propto n$$

Therefore, the pressure ratio will be equal to the ratio of the number of moles of the two gases. First, we need to find the number of moles of each gas.

The number of moles is calculated as:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

where $ m $ is the mass and $ M $ is the molar mass.

For methane, $ \text{CH}_4 $:

$$M_{\mathrm{CH}_4} = 12 + 4 \times 1 = 16 \mathrm{\ g/mol}$$

$$n_{\mathrm{CH}_4} = \frac{10 \mathrm{\ g}}{16 \mathrm{\ g/mol}} = 0.625 \mathrm{\ mol}$$

For oxygen, $ \mathrm{O}_2 $:

$$M_{\mathrm{O}_2} = 2 \times 16 = 32 \mathrm{\ g/mol}$$

$$n_{\mathrm{O}_2} = \frac{10 \mathrm{\ g}}{32 \mathrm{\ g/mol}} = 0.3125 \mathrm{\ mol}$$

Now, we calculate the pressure ratio:

$$\frac{P_{\mathrm{CH}_4}}{P_{\mathrm{O}_2}} = \frac{n_{\mathrm{CH}_4}}{n_{\mathrm{O}_2}} = \frac{0.625}{0.3125} = 2$$

Therefore, the pressure ratio of $ \mathrm{CH}_4 $ to $ \mathrm{O}_2 $ is 2:1.

The correct option is Option D.

When it reacts with these compounds, it typically produces a deep purple color known as Ruhemann's purple. Therefore, a negative test with ninhydrin implies that the compound does not contain free amino groups, ruling out amino acids (Option A) and proteins (Option D).

2. Positive test with Benedict's solution: Benedict's solution is used to test for the presence of reducing sugars. If the compound is a reducing sugar, it will reduce the copper(II) ions in Benedict's solution to form a brick-red precipitate of copper(I) oxide when heated. Therefore, a positive test with Benedict's solution indicates the presence of a reducing sugar.

Given these observations, the compound must be a reducing sugar. Thus, the correct answer is:

Option B: a monosaccharide.

en.content: A compound formed by elements A and B crystallises in the cubic structure where A atoms are at the corners of a cube and B atoms are at the face centre. The formula of the compound is :
en.explanation:
To determine the formula of the compound, we need to calculate the number of atoms of each type (A and B) in the unit cell of the crystal structure.

Firstly, let’s consider the A atoms which are located at the corners of the cube. In a cubic structure, each corner atom is shared among 8 adjacent unit cells. Thus, each corner contributes only $$\frac{1}{8}$$ of an atom to a single unit cell. Since there are 8 corners in a cube:

$$\text{Number of A atoms} = 8 \times \frac{1}{8} = 1$$

Next, consider the B atoms which are located at the face centers of the cube. Each face-centered atom is shared between 2 adjacent unit cells, therefore each face-centered atom contributes $$\frac{1}{2}$$ of an atom to a single unit cell. Since there are 6 faces in a cube:

$$\text{Number of B atoms} = 6 \times \frac{1}{2} = 3$$

Thus, in one unit cell, there is 1 A atom and 3 B atoms. Therefore, the formula of the compound is:

$$\text{AB}_3$$

So the correct answer is:

Option A: AB3

en.content: The concentration of hydrogen ion in a sample of soft drink is $$3.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$$. What is its $$\mathrm{pH}$$ ?
en.explanation:
The pH of a solution is calculated using the hydrogen ion concentration $$[H^+]$$ with the following formula:

$$\mathrm{pH} = -\log[H^+]$$

Given the hydrogen ion concentration in the sample of soft drink is $$3.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$$.

To find the pH, we substitute the given $$[H^+]$$ value into the formula:

$$\mathrm{pH} = -\log(3.8 \times 10^{-3})$$

Breaking it down, we have:

$$\mathrm{pH} = -\left(\log(3.8) + \log(10^{-3})\right)$$

Recall that $$\log(10^{-3}) = -3$$, thus:

$$\mathrm{pH} = -\left(\log(3.8) - 3\right)$$

$$\mathrm{pH} = -\log(3.8) + 3$$

Now, finding the logarithm value of 3.8 using a calculator:

$$\log(3.8) \approx 0.58$$

Hence:

$$\mathrm{pH} = -0.58 + 3$$

$$\mathrm{pH} \approx 2.42$$

Thus, the pH of the soft drink is approximately 2.42.

Therefore, the correct option is: Option B (2.42)

en.content: If $$20 \mathrm{~g}$$ of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$ is treated with $$100 \mathrm{~mL}$$ of $$20 \%$$ $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ solution, the amount of $$\mathrm{CO}_2$$ produced is
en.explanation:
Let's first write the balanced chemical equation for the reaction between calcium carbonate ($$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$) and hydrochloric acid ($$\mathrm{HCl}$$).

$$ \mathrm{CaCO}_3 + 2\mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_2 + \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} + \mathrm{CO}_2 $$

From the equation, we can see that 1 mole of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$ reacts with 2 moles of $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ to produce 1 mole of $$\mathrm{CO}_2$$.

Now, let's calculate the moles of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$. The molar mass of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$ is:

$$ \text{Molar mass of} \ \mathrm{CaCO}_3 = 40 + 12 + 3 \times 16 = 100 \ \mathrm{g/mol} $$

Given that we have $$20 \ \mathrm{g}$$ of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$, the number of moles of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$ is:

$$ \text{Moles of} \ \mathrm{CaCO}_3 = \frac{20 \ \mathrm{g}}{100 \ \mathrm{g/mol}} = 0.2 \ \mathrm{mol} $$

Now, let's determine the moles of $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ in the given solution. The solution is $$20\%$$ $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ by weight, and we have $$100 \ \mathrm{mL}$$ of the solution. Assuming the density of the solution is approximately $$1 \ \mathrm{g/mL}$$, we get:

$$ \text{Mass of} \ \mathrm{HCl} \ \text{solution} = 100 \ \mathrm{mL} \times 1 \ \mathrm{g/mL} = 100 \ \mathrm{g} $$

Since the solution is $$20\%$$ $$\mathrm{HCl}$$, the mass of $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ is:

$$ \text{Mass of} \ \mathrm{HCl} = 20\% \ \text{of} \ 100 \ \mathrm{g} = 20 \ \mathrm{g} $$

The molar mass of $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ is:

$$ \text{Molar mass of} \ \mathrm{HCl} = 1 + 35.5 = 36.5 \ \mathrm{g/mol} $$

The number of moles of $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ is:

$$ \text{Moles of} \ \mathrm{HCl} = \frac{20 \ \mathrm{g}}{36.5 \ \mathrm{g/mol}} \approx 0.548 \ \mathrm{mol} $$

The reaction requires 2 moles of $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ for 1 mole of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$. Since we have only $$0.2 \ \mathrm{mol}$$ of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$, we need:

$$ \text{Required moles of} \ \mathrm{HCl} = 0.2 \ \mathrm{mol} \times 2 = 0.4 \ \mathrm{mol} $$

We have more than enough $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ ($$0.548 \ \mathrm{mol}$$ available) to fully react with $$0.2 \ \mathrm{mol}$$ of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$.

Therefore, the amount of $$\mathrm{CO}_2$$ produced will be equal to the moles of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$ reacted, which is $$0.2 \ \mathrm{mol}$$.

To find the mass of $$\mathrm{CO}_2$$ produced, we use its molar mass:

$$ \text{Molar mass of} \ \mathrm{CO}_2 = 12 + 2 \times 16 = 44 \ \mathrm{g/mol} $$

The mass of $$\mathrm{CO}_2$$ is:

$$ \text{Mass of} \ \mathrm{CO}_2 = 0.2 \ \mathrm{mol} \times 44 \ \mathrm{g/mol} = 8.80 \ \mathrm{g} $$

The amount of $$\mathrm{CO}_2$$ produced is $$8.80 \ \mathrm{g}$$.

Given the options, the correct answer is:

Option B - 8.80 g

en.content: Which of the following acts as interstitial hydride?
en.explanation:
The correct answer is Option C: ZnH2. Here's why:

Interstitial hydrides are metallic hydrides formed when hydrogen atoms occupy the interstitial spaces (voids) within the crystal lattice of a transition metal. These hydrides are typically non-stoichiometric, meaning the ratio of metal to hydrogen atoms is not fixed and can vary depending on the conditions.

Let's analyze the options:

A) NH3 (Ammonia): Ammonia is a covalent compound with a strong N-H bond. It does not form an interstitial hydride.

B) CH4 (Methane): Methane is a covalent compound with strong C-H bonds. It does not form an interstitial hydride.

C) ZnH2 (Zinc Hydride): Zinc is a transition metal, and its hydride, ZnH2, is a classic example of an interstitial hydride. Hydrogen atoms occupy the interstitial sites within the zinc crystal lattice.

D) H2O (Water): Water is a covalent compound with strong O-H bonds. It does not form an interstitial hydride.

Therefore, only ZnH2 fits the definition of an interstitial hydride.

en.content: Which of the following statements does not form a part of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom?

options.content: The position and velocity of the electrons in the orbit cannot be determined simultaneously.
The electron in the orbit nearest the nucleus has the lowest energy.
Electrons revolve in different orbits around the nucleus.
Energy of the electrons in the orbit is quantised.

en.explanation:
Bohr's model of the hydrogen atom primarily describes the behavior of electrons in fixed orbits around the nucleus with quantized energy levels. Below is an analysis of each statement:

Option A: The position and velocity of the electrons in the orbit cannot be determined simultaneously.

This statement refers to the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle and is not a part of Bohr's model. The Bohr model assumes electrons have well-defined orbits with known velocities and positions.

Option B: The electron in the orbit nearest the nucleus has the lowest energy.

This is indeed a part of Bohr's model. Bohr postulated that electrons occupy specific energy levels, and the closest orbit to the nucleus has the least energy.

Option C: Electrons revolve in different orbits around the nucleus.

This statement is a fundamental aspect of Bohr's model, where electrons move in discrete orbits or energy levels around the nucleus.

Option D: Energy of the electrons in the orbit is quantised.

This statement is also a key component of Bohr's model. Bohr proposed that the energy of electrons in these orbits is quantized, meaning electrons can only occupy certain energy levels.

Given the analysis, the statement that does not form a part of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom is:

Option A: The position and velocity of the electrons in the orbit cannot be determined simultaneously.

en.content:
The equilibrium constant of the reaction

$$A(s)+2 B^{+}(a q) \rightleftharpoons A^{2+}(a q)+2 B(s)$$

$$E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=0.0295 \mathrm{~V} \text { is } \quad\left[\frac{2.303 R T}{F}=0.059\right]$$
en.explanation:
To find the equilibrium constant $$K_{eq}$$ for the given reaction, we can use the relation between the standard cell potential $$E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ}$$ and the equilibrium constant. The Nernst equation at standard conditions can be written as:

$$E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} = \frac{0.059}{n} \log K_{eq}$$

Here:

   $$E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} = 0.0295\, \text{V}$$

   $$n = 2$$ (number of electrons transferred in the balanced reaction)

   $$\frac{2.303RT}{F} = 0.059$$

Now, solving for $$K_{eq}$$:

$$0.0295 = \frac{0.059}{2} \log K_{eq}$$

Multiplying both sides by 2 to isolate the logarithm term:

$$0.059 = 0.059 \log K_{eq}$$

Dividing both sides by 0.059:

$$1 = \log K_{eq}$$

Taking the antilog (base 10) of both sides:

$$K_{eq} = 10^1 = 10$$

So, the correct answer is Option D: 10.

en.content: The value of enthalpy change $$(\Delta H)$$ for the reaction $$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH}(l)+3 \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+ 3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l)$$, at $$27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ is $$-1366 \cdot 5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$. The value of internal energy change for the above reaction at this temperature will be
en.explanation:
To find the value of the internal energy change $$(\Delta U)$$ for the given reaction at $$27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$, we can use the relationship between enthalpy change $$(\Delta H)$$ and internal energy change $$(\Delta U)$$, given by the equation:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n \cdot R \cdot T$$

Where:

  • $$\Delta H$$ is the enthalpy change
  • $$\Delta U$$ is the internal energy change
  • $$\Delta n$$ is the change in the number of moles of gas
  • $$R$$ is the gas constant, which is $$8.314~\mathrm{J~mol^{-1}~K^{-1}}$$
  • $$T$$ is the temperature in Kelvin

First, we need to determine $$\Delta n$$, the change in the number of moles of gas during the reaction:

For the reaction:

$$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH}(l)+3 \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+ 3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l)$$

Moles of gas on the reactant side = 3 (from $$\mathrm{O}_2$$)

Moles of gas on the product side = 2 (from $$\mathrm{CO}_2$$)

So,

$$\Delta n = \text{{moles of gas on product side}} - \text{{moles of gas on reactant side}} = 2 - 3 = -1$$

Now, we can convert the temperature to Kelvin:

$$T = 27^{\circ} \mathrm{C} + 273 = 300~\mathrm{K}$$

Next, we substitute the values into the equation:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n \cdot R \cdot T$$

Here,

$$\Delta H = -1366.5~\mathrm{kJ~mol^{-1}} = -1366.5 \cdot 10^3~\mathrm{J~mol^{-1}}$$

$$\Delta n = -1$$

$$R = 8.314~\mathrm{J~mol^{-1}~K^{-1}}$$

$$T = 300~\mathrm{K}$$

Solve for $$\Delta U$$:

$$-1366.5 \cdot 10^3 = \Delta U + (-1) \cdot 8.314 \cdot 300$$

Simplify the equation:

$$-1366.5 \cdot 10^3 = \Delta U - 2494.2$$

So,

$$\Delta U = -1366.5 \cdot 10^3 + 2494.2$$

$$\Delta U \approx -1366.5 \cdot 10^3 + 2.494 \cdot 10^3$$

$$\Delta U \approx -1364.0 \cdot 10^3~\mathrm{J~mol^{-1}}$$

Therefore, the internal energy change $$\Delta U$$ is:

$$\Delta U \approx -1364.0~\mathrm{kJ~mol^{-1}}$$

Hence, the correct option is:

Option C: $$-1364.0~\mathrm{kJ~mol^{-1}}$$

en.content: If two compounds have the same empirical formula but different molecular formulae, they must have
en.explanation:
If two compounds have the same empirical formula but different molecular formulae, they must have different molecular weights. The empirical formula represents the simplest whole-number ratio of the elements in the compound, but it doesn't provide information about the actual number of atoms in a molecule (molecular formula). Because the molecular formula is a multiple of the empirical formula, compounds with different molecular formulae will have different molecular weights even if their empirical formula is the same.

To illustrate with an example, consider the empirical formula $$CH_2$$. This empirical formula can correspond to several different molecular formulae such as $$C_2H_4$$, $$C_3H_6$$, $$C_4H_8$$, and so on. Each of these molecular formulae has a different molecular weight, even though they share the same empirical formula.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: different molecular weights

en.content: Which of the following is cross-linked polymer?
en.explanation:
A cross-linked polymer is a type of polymer where the individual polymer chains are linked together by covalent bonds, forming a three-dimensional network. This cross-linking process significantly affects the properties of the polymer, making it more rigid, durable, and resistant to heat and chemicals.

Let's evaluate the given options:

Option A: Teflon

Teflon is a brand name for a polymer called polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE). It is not a cross-linked polymer; rather, it consists of linear chains without cross-linking.

Option B: Polythene

Polythene or polyethylene is a polymer made from ethylene monomers. It can be either low-density polyethylene (LDPE), which has some branching, or high-density polyethylene (HDPE), which is mostly linear. Neither form is typically cross-linked.

Option C: Nylon

Nylon is a type of synthetic polymer, specifically a polyamide. Nylon polymers are generally linear and not cross-linked.

Option D: Bakelite

Bakelite is a type of phenol-formaldehyde resin, which is a thermosetting polymer. Thermosetting polymers are known for their heavily cross-linked structures. Bakelite forms an extensive, three-dimensional network through cross-linking, which makes it very hard and heat-resistant.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: Bakelite

en.content: In electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction, the nitro group is meta-directing because it

options.content: decreases electron density at ortho-and para-positions
increases electron density at ortho-and para-positions

en.explanation:
In electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) reactions, the nitro group ($$ \text{NO}_2 $$) is known to be a strong electron-withdrawing group due to its resonance and inductive effects. This property significantly influences the electron distribution in the aromatic ring, thereby affecting the position where incoming electrophiles are most likely to attack.

Let's analyze the options provided in this context:

  • Option A: Decreases electron density at ortho-and para-positions
  • Option B: Decreases electron density at meta-position
  • Option C: Increases electron density at meta-position
  • Option D: Increases electron density at ortho-and para-positions

The nitro group, being electron-withdrawing, pulls electron density away from the aromatic ring through two main mechanisms:

  • Inductive Effect: The nitro group withdraws electron density through the sigma bonds due to its high electronegativity.
  • Resonance Effect: The nitro group can participate in resonance, pulling electron density away from the ring, particularly affecting the ortho and para positions relative to the nitro group.

Due to the electron-withdrawing nature of the nitro group, electron density is significantly reduced at the ortho and para positions more than the meta positions. This makes the ortho and para positions less reactive towards electrophiles since electrophiles are attracted to electron-rich areas.

As a result, the meta position remains relatively electron-rich in comparison to the ortho and para positions. Thus, the electrophilic substitution is more likely to occur at the meta position.

Based on this understanding, the correct answer is:

Option A: Decreases electron density at ortho-and para-positions

en.content: The correct statement with respect to the complexes $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$ and $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$ is

options.content: nickel is in the same oxidation state in both.
have square planar and tetrahedral geometry, respectively.
have tetrahedral and square planar geometry, respectively.

en.explanation:
To determine the correct statement with respect to the complexes $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$ and $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$, we need to analyze their coordination, oxidation states, and molecular geometries.

Option A: Nickel is in the same oxidation state in both.

To find the oxidation state of Ni in $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$, we consider that CO is a neutral ligand. Therefore, the oxidation state of Ni here is +0.

In $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$, since CN is a -1 ligand and the overall charge on the complex is -2, the oxidation state of Ni is +2.

So, nickel is not in the same oxidation state in both complexes.

Option B: Both have square planar geometry.

To determine the geometry, we need to consider the electronic configuration and the nature of the ligands:

  • $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$: This complex has Ni in the 0 oxidation state with an electronic configuration of $$3d^8 4s^2$$. CO is a strong field ligand but with a $$d^10$$ configuration (Ni(0)), leading to a tetrahedral geometry.
  • $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$: This complex has Ni in the +2 oxidation state with an electronic configuration of $$3d^8$$. CN is a strong field ligand, causing a square planar geometry due to the dsp² hybridization to minimize repulsion.

Thus, both do not have square planar geometry.

Option C: Have square planar and tetrahedral geometry, respectively.

Based on our analysis of the electronic configuration and hybridization, it is clear that $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$ has a tetrahedral geometry and $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$ has a square planar geometry.

Hence, this statement is incorrect because it is the opposite of our findings.

Option D: Have tetrahedral and square planar geometry, respectively.

From the above analysis:

  • $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$: Tetrahedral geometry.
  • $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$: Square planar geometry.

This statement is correct based on our conclusions.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option D: Have tetrahedral and square planar geometry, respectively.

en.content: Sulphur does not exist as $$\mathrm{S}_2$$ molecule because

options.content: it is not able to constitute $$p \pi-p \pi$$ bonds
it has ability to exhibit catenation
of tendency to show variable oxidation states

en.explanation:
Sulphur does not exist as $$\mathrm{S}_2$$ molecule primarily because of its inability to constitute $$p \pi-p \pi$$ bonds.

In the case of oxygen, the small size of $$O$$ atoms and the high electronegativity allow the $$2p$$ orbitals to overlap effectively, forming a strong $$p \pi-p \pi$$ double bond in $$\mathrm{O}_2$$. As a result, molecular oxygen exists as a diatomic molecule. However, sulphur atoms are larger and less electronegative compared to oxygen. This significant size difference makes the overlap between $$3p$$ orbitals (the corresponding orbitals in sulphur) less effective for forming strong $$p \pi-p \pi$$ double bonds.

Instead, sulphur stabilizes itself more effectively by catenating to form larger molecular structures like $$\mathrm{S}_8$$, where each sulphur atom forms single bonds with adjacent sulphur atoms, creating a puckered ring structure. This results in the elemental sulphur commonly found in the form of octa-atomic rings.

So, the correct answer is:

Option A: it is not able to constitute $$p \pi-p \pi$$ bonds

en.content: A mixture of two moles of carbon monoxide and one mole of oxygen, in a closed vessel is ignited to convert the carbon monoxide to carbon dioxide. If $$\Delta H$$ is the enthalpy change and $$\Delta E$$ is the change in internal energy, then

options.content: the relationship depends on the capacity of the vessel

en.explanation:
To determine the relationship between the enthalpy change ($$\Delta H$$) and the internal energy change ($$\Delta E$$) for the given reaction, we need to examine the process and consider the relevant thermodynamic equations.

The reaction given is the combustion of carbon monoxide:

$$ 2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 $$

We know that this reaction occurs in a closed vessel, which implies a constant volume process. The relationship between enthalpy change and internal energy change for a reaction can be described by the equation:

$$ \Delta H = \Delta E + \Delta n_gRT $$

Where:

  • $$\Delta H$$ is the enthalpy change
  • $$\Delta E$$ is the internal energy change
  • $$\Delta n_g$$ is the change in the number of moles of gases
  • $$R$$ is the universal gas constant
  • $$T$$ is the temperature in Kelvin

To determine $$\Delta n_g$$, we need to look at the number of moles of gas before and after the reaction:

  • Before the reaction: 2 moles of CO and 1 mole of $$\text{O}_2$$, totaling 3 moles of gas.
  • After the reaction: 2 moles of $$\text{CO}_2$$.

Hence, $$\Delta n_g$$ can be calculated as:

$$ \Delta n_g = n_{\text{final}} - n_{\text{initial}} = 2 - 3 = -1 $$

Substituting $$\Delta n_g = -1$$ into the enthalpy-internal energy relationship:

$$ \Delta H = \Delta E + (-1)RT $$

$$ \Delta H = \Delta E - RT $$

Since $$RT$$ is always a positive quantity, it means that:

$$ \Delta H < \Delta E $$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option B: $$\Delta H < \Delta U$$

en.content: Which one of the following decreasing orders of stability is correct?
en.explanation:
The correct order of decreasing stability of carbonates is:

$$ \mathrm{BaCO}_3 > \mathrm{SrCO}_3 > \mathrm{CaCO}_3 > \mathrm{MgCO}_3$$

Therefore, the answer is Option B.

Let's understand why this is the case. The stability of carbonates is related to the size of the metal cation. As we move down the group in the periodic table, the size of the metal cation increases. A larger cation has a weaker attraction to the carbonate anion. This weaker attraction makes the carbonate less stable.

Here's a breakdown:

  • Ba2+ is the largest cation and therefore has the weakest attraction to the carbonate anion, making BaCO3 the most stable.

  • Sr2+ is smaller than Ba2+, leading to a stronger attraction and less stability compared to BaCO3.

  • Ca2+ is smaller than Sr2+, making CaCO3 less stable than SrCO3.

  • Mg2+ is the smallest cation, resulting in the strongest attraction and the least stable carbonate, MgCO3.

en.content: Which of the following is added to remove permanent hardness of water?
en.explanation:
Permanent hardness of water is caused by the presence of soluble salts of calcium and magnesium, such as calcium chloride ($$\mathrm{CaCl}_2$$) and magnesium sulfate ($$\mathrm{MgSO}_4$$). These salts cannot be removed by simple boiling. One effective method to remove permanent hardness is to convert these soluble salts into insoluble compounds. This can be achieved by adding certain chemicals to the water that react with the calcium and magnesium ions to form precipitates.

Sodium carbonate ($$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$$), also known as washing soda, is commonly added for this purpose. When sodium carbonate is added to hard water, it reacts with the calcium and magnesium ions to form insoluble carbonates, which can then be removed by filtration. The reactions are as follows:

$$\mathrm{Ca}^{2+} + \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + 2\mathrm{Na}^+$$

$$\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} + \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{MgCO}_3 \downarrow + 2\mathrm{Na}^+$$

In these reactions, calcium and magnesium ions combine with carbonate ions from sodium carbonate to form insoluble calcium carbonate ($$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$) and magnesium carbonate ($$\mathrm{MgCO}_3$$) precipitates, respectively.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option B

$$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$$

en.content:
Identify product $$(Z)$$ in the series, of reaction

$$\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\mathrm{HBr}} X \xrightarrow{a q . \mathrm{KOH}} Y \xrightarrow[\mathrm{I}_2 \text { excess }]{\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3} Z$$
en.explanation:
Let's go through the sequence of reactions step by step to identify the final product $$(Z)$$.

Step 1: $$\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\mathrm{HBr}} X$$

The addition of hydrogen bromide (HBr) to ethene ($$\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}_2$$) follows Markovnikov's rule, forming bromoethane ($$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Br}$$) as the product.

$$\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}_2 + \mathrm{HBr} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Br}$$

So, $$X$$ is $$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Br}$$.

Step 2: $$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Br} \xrightarrow{a q . \mathrm{KOH}} Y$$

When bromoethane reacts with aqueous potassium hydroxide (KOH), nucleophilic substitution occurs, converting bromoethane to ethanol ($$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH}$$).

$$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Br} + \mathrm{KOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH} + \mathrm{KBr}$$

So, $$Y$$ is $$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH}$$.

Step 3: $$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{I}_2 \text{ excess }]{\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3} Z$$

Ethanol (ethyl alcohol) reacts with iodine ($$\mathrm{I}_2$$) in the presence of sodium carbonate ($$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$$) to form iodoform ($$\mathrm{CHI}_3$$). This reaction is known as the iodoform test, which is a common qualitative test for the presence of methyl ketones and ethanol.

$$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH} + 4\mathrm{I}_2 + \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{CHI}_3 + 5\mathrm{NaI} + \mathrm{HCOONa} + 2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$$

So, $$Z$$ is $$\mathrm{CHI}_3$$ (iodoform).

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option C: $$\mathrm{CHI}_3$$

en.content: The temperature of $$K$$ at which $$\Delta G=0$$, for a given reaction with $$\Delta H=-20.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$ and $$\Delta S=-50.0 \mathrm{~JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$ is
en.explanation:
To determine the temperature $$K$$ at which $$\Delta G = 0$$ for a given reaction, we use the relationship between the Gibbs free energy change ($$\Delta G$$), enthalpy change ($$\Delta H$$), and entropy change ($$\Delta S$$), expressed by the Gibbs free energy equation:

$$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S $$

Given that $$\Delta G = 0$$, we set the equation as follows:

$$ 0 = \Delta H - T \Delta S $$

Solving for the temperature $$T$$, we get:

$$ T = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} $$

Now, we can plug in the given values:

$$ \Delta H = -20.5 \ \mathrm{~kJ} \ \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} = -20500 \ \mathrm{~J} \ \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} $$

$$ \Delta S = -50.0 \ \mathrm{~JK}^{-1} \ \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} $$

Substituting these values into the equation:

$$ T = \frac{-20500 \ \mathrm{~J} \ \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{-50.0 \ \mathrm{~JK}^{-1} \ \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}} $$

$$ T = \frac{20500}{50.0} $$

$$ T = 410 \ \mathrm{K} $$

Therefore, the temperature $$K$$ at which $$\Delta G = 0$$ is 410 K. The correct answer is:

Option B

410

en.content:
Which one of the following is/are linear structure?

I. $$\mathrm{I}_3^{-}$$

II. $$\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}$$

III. $$\mathrm{I}_3^{+}$$

IV. $$\mathrm{SO}_2$$

V. $$\mathrm{N}_3^{-}$$
en.explanation:
$$\mathrm{I}_3^{-}$$ ion is made up of an $$\mathrm{I}_2$$ molecule with an $$\mathrm{I}^{-}$$ bonded to it by means of a coordinate bond in which $$\mathrm{I}_2$$ is lone pair acceptor (Lewis acid) and $$I^{-}$$ is lone pair donor (Lewis base).

There are two bond pairs and three lone pairs in the outer shell of central atom. To minimise the repulsive forces the three lone pairs occupy the equatorial position.

The ion is therefore, linear in shape with a bond angle of exactly $$180^{\circ}$$.

$$ \underset{\substack{\text { Lewis } \\ \text { acid }}}{\mathrm{I}_2}+\underset{\substack{\text { Lewis } \\ \text { base }}}{\mathrm{I}^{-}} \longrightarrow[\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{I} \leftarrow \mathrm{I}]^{-}$$

Similarly, $$\mathrm{N}_3^{-}$$ ion is also linear in shape. $$\mathrm{I}_3^{+}$$ does not have linear structure.

en.content: If $$\alpha<1$$ be a root of the equation $$2 x^2-5 x+2=0$$, then the other root of the equation is
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } 2 x^2-5 x+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 x^2-4 x-x+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 x(x-2)-1(x-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(2 x-1)(x-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{1}{2}, 2 \quad \therefore \quad \alpha=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \text { Now, } 4 \alpha^2+4 \alpha=4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=1+2=3 \neq 2 \\ & 4 \alpha^3-3 \alpha=4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3-3\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2}=-1 \neq 2 \\ & 4 \alpha^2-2 \alpha=4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2-2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=1-1=0 \neq 2 \\ & 4 \alpha^3+3 \alpha=4\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3+3\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{3}{2}=2 \end{aligned}$$

Thus, other root be $$4 \alpha^3+3 \alpha$$.

en.content: A six faced die is a biased once. It is thrice more likely to show an odd number, then show an even number. It is thrown twice. The probability that the sum of the number in two throws is odd, is
en.explanation:
Let $$P$$ be the probability of getting an even number. Then, by hypothesis the probability of getting an odd number be $$3 P$$.

Since, event of getting an odd number and an even number are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.

$$\therefore \quad 3 P+P=1 \Rightarrow P=\frac{1}{4}$$

Thus, the probability of getting an odd number in a single throw is $$\frac{3}{4}$$ and that of an even number is $$\frac{1}{4}$$. If the die is thrown twice, then the sum of the numbers in two throws is odd if one number is odd and another is even.

$$\therefore$$ Required probability

$$=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{3}{4}+\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{6}{16}=\frac{3}{8}$$

en.content: Number of solutions of the equation $$z^2+|z|^2=0$$ and $$z \neq 0$$ is
en.explanation:
Given, $$z^2+|z|^2=0, z \neq 0$$

Let $$z=x+i y$$ and $$\bar{z}=x-i y$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow(x+i y)^2+(x+i y)(x-i y)=0 & \left\{\because z \bar{z}=|z|^2\right\} \\ \Rightarrow x^2+i^2 y^2+i 2 x y+x^2-i^2 y^2=0 \\ \Rightarrow x^2-y^2+i 2 x y+x^2+y^2=0 \quad \{\because i^2=-1\}\\ \Rightarrow 2 x(x+i y)=0 \\ \Rightarrow x=0 \text { or } x+i y=0 \text { (not possible) } \end{array}$$

Therefore, $$x=0$$ and $$y \neq 0$$

So, $$y$$ can take any non-zero real value.

Hence, infinitely many solutions exists.

en.content: If $$y=m_1 x+c_1$$ and $$y=m_2 x+c_2, m_1 \neq m_2$$ are two common tangents of circle $$x^2+y^2=2$$ and parabola $$y^2=x$$, then the value of $$8\left|m_1 m_2\right|$$ is equal to
en.explanation:
Given, $$y=m_1 x+c_1$$ is common tangent of circle and parabola.

For circle, $$\left|\frac{c_1}{\sqrt{1+m_1^2}}\right|=\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow c_1^2=2\left(1+m_1^2\right)$$ .... (i)

For parabola, $$c_1=\frac{1}{4 m_1} \Rightarrow c_1^2=\frac{1}{16 m_1^2}$$ .... (ii)

Also, $$y=m_2 x+c_2$$ is common tangent of circle and parabola.

For circle, $$\left|\frac{c_2}{\sqrt{1+m_2^2}}\right|=\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow c_2^2=2\left(1+m_2^2\right)$$

For parabola, $$c_2=\frac{1}{4 m_2} \Rightarrow c_2^2=\frac{1}{16 m_2^2}$$

Now, $$2\left(1+m_1^2\right)=\frac{1}{16 m_1^2} \quad$$ [From Eqs. (i) and (ii)]

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad 32 m_1^4+32 m_1^2-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad m_1^2=\frac{-32 \pm \sqrt{(32)^2-4(32)(-1)}}{2 \times 32} \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad m_1^2=\frac{-32+24 \sqrt{2}}{64}$$

[Ignoring negative value because $$m^2 \geq 0$$]

$$\Rightarrow m_1= \pm \sqrt{\frac{-32+24 \sqrt{2}}{64}}$$

Similarly, $$m_2= \pm \sqrt{\frac{-32+24 \sqrt{2}}{64}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore 8\left|m_1 m_2\right| =8\left|\left( \pm \sqrt{\frac{-32+24 \sqrt{2}}{64}}\right)\left( \pm \sqrt{\frac{-32+24 \sqrt{2}}{64}}\right)\right| \\ =\frac{8}{64}(-32+24 \sqrt{2}) \\ =-4+3 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If $$A=\{x: x$$ is a multiple of 8$$\}$$ and $$B=\{x: x$$ is a multiple of 12$$\}$$, then $$A \cap B$$ consists of multiple of
en.explanation:
To determine the set $$A \cap B$$, which represents the common elements between sets $$A$$ and $$B$$, we need to find the multiples of both 8 and 12. In other words, we need to find the least common multiples (LCM) of 8 and 12.

First, let's find the prime factorization of 8 and 12:

- 8 can be factored into prime numbers as: $$8 = 2^3$$

- 12 can be factored into prime numbers as: $$12 = 2^2 \cdot 3$$

To find the LCM, we take the highest power of each prime that appears in these factorizations. Thus, the LCM of 8 and 12 is:

$$\text{LCM}(8, 12) = 2^3 \cdot 3 = 8 \cdot 3 = 24$$

Therefore, the multiples of 8 and 12 are common multiples of 24. Consequently, the set $$A \cap B$$, which consists of numbers that are multiples of both 8 and 12, consists of multiples of 24.

The correct option is:

Option D: 24

en.content: If the system of linear equation $$3 x-2 y+z=2, 4 x-3 y+3 z=-5$$ and $$7 x-5 y+\lambda z=9$$ has no solution, then $$\lambda$$ equals to
en.explanation:
To determine the value of $$\lambda$$ for which the given system of linear equations has no solution, we need to analyze the consistency of these equations. The given system is:

$$3x - 2y + z = 2$$ (Equation 1)

$$4x - 3y + 3z = -5$$ (Equation 2)

$$7x - 5y + \lambda z = 9$$ (Equation 3)

For a system of linear equations to have no solution, the equations must be inconsistent, meaning they do not intersect at any point. This happens when the determinant of the coefficient matrix is zero, and the augmented matrix creates a contradiction.

First, let's form the coefficient matrix and calculate its determinant. The coefficient matrix $$A$$ of the system is:

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -2 & 1 \\ 4 & -3 & 3 \\ 7 & -5 & \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$

The determinant of matrix $$A$$ is given by:

$$\text{det}(A) = \begin{vmatrix} 3 & -2 & 1 \\ 4 & -3 & 3 \\ 7 & -5 & \lambda \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding this determinant along the first row, we get:

$$\text{det}(A) = 3\begin{vmatrix} -3 & 3 \\ -5 & \lambda \end{vmatrix} - (-2)\begin{vmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 7 & \lambda \end{vmatrix} + 1\begin{vmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ 7 & -5 \end{vmatrix}$$

Now, calculate these 2x2 determinants:

$$\begin{vmatrix} -3 & 3 \\ -5 & \lambda \end{vmatrix} = (-3)(\lambda) - (3)(-5) = -3\lambda + 15$$

$$\begin{vmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 7 & \lambda \end{vmatrix} = (4)(\lambda) - (3)(7) = 4\lambda - 21$$

$$\begin{vmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ 7 & -5 \end{vmatrix} = (4)(-5) - (-3)(7) = -20 + 21 = 1$$

Substitute these into the expansion to get the determinant:

$$\text{det}(A) = 3(-3\lambda + 15) + 2(4\lambda - 21) + 1(1)$$

$$= 3(-3\lambda + 15) + 2(4\lambda - 21) + 1$$

$$= 3(-3\lambda) + 3(15) + 2(4\lambda) - 2(21) + 1$$

$$= -9\lambda + 45 + 8\lambda - 42 + 1$$

$$= -\lambda + 4$$

For the system to have no solution, $$\text{det}(A)$$ must be zero, because that's when the coefficient matrix does not have full rank and the system is inconsistent:

$$-\lambda + 4 = 0$$

$$\lambda = 4$$

Therefore, the value of $$\lambda$$ that makes the system of linear equations have no solution is $$4$$.

Answer: (A) 4

en.content: If $$\cot ^{-1} \sqrt{\cos \alpha}-\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\cos \alpha}=x$$, then $$\sin x$$ is equal to
en.explanation:
Given, $$\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})-\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\cos \alpha}=x$$ ... (i)

We know that $$\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos } \alpha)+\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\cos \alpha}=\frac{\pi}{2}$$ .... (ii)

On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 \cot ^{-1} \sqrt{\cos \alpha}=\frac{\pi}{2}+x \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\cos } \alpha=\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)=\frac{\cot \frac{x}{2}-1}{1+\cot \frac{x}{2}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow \sqrt{\cos \alpha}=\frac{\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}}{\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}}$$

On squaring both sides, we get

$$\cos \alpha=\frac{\cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}+\sin ^2 \frac{x}{2}-2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}{\cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}+\sin ^2 \frac{x}{2}+2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \cos \alpha=\frac{1-\sin x}{1+\sin x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1-\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}}{1+\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}}=\frac{1-\sin x}{1+\sin x} \end{aligned}$$

On applying componendo and dividendo rule, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1-\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}-1-\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}}{1-\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}+1+\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}}=\frac{1-\sin x-1-\sin x}{1-\sin x+1+\sin x} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}=\sin x \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \sin x=\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2} \end{aligned}$$

en.explanation:
The normal line will makes $$45^{\circ}$$ angle with positive $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis because it is inclined at $$45^{\circ}$$ with the straight line $$y=6$$ which is parallel to the $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis.

$$\therefore$$ Slope of normal line $$=m=\tan 45^{\circ}=1$$

The centre of the circle, $$x^2+y^2=25$$ is $$(0,0)$$.

So, the normal line will passes through $$(0,0)$$, because normal line at any point $$P$$ on the circle passes through its centre.

Thus, the equation of normal line be $$y=x$$ and the point $$P$$ lies on the straight line $$y=x$$.

en.content: Let $$\frac{1}{16}, a$$ and $$b$$ be in GP and $$\frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{b}, 6$$ be in AP, where $$a, b>0$$. Then, $$72(a+b)$$ is equal to
en.explanation:
To solve the problem, let’s analyze it step by step.

Firstly, since $$\frac{1}{16}, a,$$ and $$b$$ are in geometric progression (GP), the common ratio can be denoted as $$r$$. For GP, the relation between terms is given by:

If $$\frac{1}{16}$$ is the first term and $$a$$ is the second term, then:

$$ a = \frac{1}{16} \cdot r $$

If $$a$$ is the second term and $$b$$ is the third term, then:

$$ b = a \cdot r $$

Substituting the expression for $$a$$ in the second equation:

$$ b = \left( \frac{1}{16} \cdot r \right) \cdot r = \frac{1}{16} \cdot r^2 $$

We are also given that $$\frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{b},$$ and $$6$$ are in arithmetic progression (AP). For AP, the middle term is the average of the two surrounding terms:

Since $$\frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{b}, 6$$ are in AP:

$$ 2 \cdot \frac{1}{b} = \frac{1}{a} + 6 $$

Now, substituting the values of $$\frac{1}{a}$$ and $$\frac{1}{b}$$ from the GP relations:

Since $$ a = \frac{1}{16} \cdot r $$:

$$ \frac{1}{a} = \frac{1}{ \left( \frac{1}{16} \cdot r \right) } = 16 \cdot \frac{1}{r} = \frac{16}{r} $$

Also, since $$ b = \frac{1}{16} \cdot r^2 $$:

$$ \frac{1}{b} = \frac{1}{ \left( \frac{1}{16} \cdot r^2 \right) } = 16 \cdot \frac{1}{r^2} = \frac{16}{r^2} $$

Substitute these values into the AP equation:

$$ 2 \cdot \frac{16}{r^2} = \frac{16}{r} + 6 $$

Expanding and simplifying, we get:

$$ \frac{32}{r^2} = \frac{16}{r} + 6 $$

To clear the fractions, multiply all terms by $$r^2$$:

$$ 32 = 16r + 6r^2 $$

Rearranging into standard quadratic form:

$$ 6r^2 + 16r - 32 = 0 $$

Divide throughout by 2 for simplicity:

$$ 3r^2 + 8r - 16 = 0 $$

Solving the quadratic equation using the quadratic formula:

$$ r = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} $$

where $$a = 3$$, $$b = 8$$, and $$c = -16$$:

$$ r = \frac{-8 \pm \sqrt{8^2 - 4 \cdot 3 \cdot (-16)}}{2 \cdot 3} = \frac{-8 \pm \sqrt{64 + 192}}{6} = \frac{-8 \pm \sqrt{256}}{6} = \frac{-8 \pm 16}{6} $$

This gives us two possible values for $$r$$:

$$ r = \frac{8}{6} = \frac{4}{3} $$

$$ r = \frac{-24}{6} = -4 $$

Since $$a$$ and $$b$$ are positive, $$r = \frac{4}{3}$$ is the suitable solution.

Now calculate values for $$a$$ and $$b$$:

$$ a = \frac{1}{16} \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{1}{12} $$

$$ b = \left( \frac{1}{12} \right) \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{1}{9} $$

Finally,$$ 72(a + b) $$ =:

$$ 72 \left( \frac{1}{12} + \frac{1}{9} \right) = 72 \left( \frac{3 + 4}{36} \right) = 72 \left( \frac{7}{36} \right) = 14 $$

Therefore, the answer is Option B: 14.

en.content: The sum of the coefficients of all odd degree terms in the expansion of $$\left(x+\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5 +\left(x-\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5, x>1$$ is
en.explanation:
We have, $$\left(x+\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5+\left(x-\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5, x>1$$

$$\begin{array}{r} =x^5+5 x^4 \sqrt{x^3-1}+10 x^3\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^2+10 x^2\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^3 \\ +5 x\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^4+\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5+\left(x^5-5 x^2 \sqrt{x^3-1}+\right. \\ 10 x^3\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^2-10 x^2\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^3+5 x\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^4 \\ -\left(\sqrt{x^3-1}\right)^5 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =2\left(x^5+10 x^3\left(x^3-1\right)+5 x\left(x^3-1\right)^2\right) \\ & =2\left(x^5+10 x^6-10 x^3+5 x^7-10 x^4+5 x\right) \end{aligned}$$

Now, the sum of coefficients of all odd degree term is

$$2(1-10+5+5)=2$$

en.explanation:

$$\begin{array}{cc} \Rightarrow & 2 \cos x\left(2 \cos (2 x)-2 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)-1\right)=1 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 \cos x\left(4 \cos ^2 x-3\right)=1 \\ \Rightarrow & 4 \cos ^3 x-3 \cos x=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & \cos 3 x=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & 3 x=\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{5 \pi}{3}, \frac{7 \pi}{3} \\ & x=\frac{\pi}{9}, \frac{5 \pi}{9}, \frac{7 \pi}{9} \end{array}$$

en.explanation:
Here, from the figure, we see that $$\theta_2=\tan ^{-1} \frac{3}{5}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta_2=\frac{3}{5} \quad \text{.... (i)}$$

$$\text { In } \triangle A O C, \tan \theta_1=\frac{A C}{A O} \Rightarrow \tan \theta_1=\frac{\frac{1}{4} h}{40}=\frac{h}{160} \quad \text{.... (ii)}$$

And in $$\triangle A O B, \tan \left(\theta_1+\theta_2\right)=\frac{A B}{A O}=\frac{h}{40}$$

$$\begin{array}{lc} \Rightarrow & \frac{\tan \theta_1+\tan \theta_2}{1-\tan \theta_1 \tan \theta_2}=\frac{h}{40} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{\frac{h}{160}+\frac{3}{5}}{1-\frac{h}{160} \times \frac{3}{5}}=\frac{h}{40} \\ \Rightarrow & h^2-200 h+6400=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (h-160)(h-40)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & h=160 \text { or } h=40 \end{array}$$

Hence, height of the vertical pole is $$40 \mathrm{~m}$$.

en.content: If $$a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$$ are in HP, then the expression $$a_1 a_2+a_2 a_3+\ldots+a_{n-1} a_n$$ is equal to
en.explanation:
Given, $$a_1, a_2, \ldots a_n$$ are in HP

$$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{a_1}, \frac{1}{a_2}, \ldots, \frac{1}{a_n}$$ are in AP

$$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{a_2}-\frac{1}{a_1}=d$$, where $$d$$ is common difference

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad a_1 a_2 & =\frac{a_1-a_2}{d}=\frac{a_1}{d}-\frac{a_2}{d} \quad \text{... (i)}\\ & a_2 a_3=\frac{a_2}{d}-\frac{a_3}{d} \quad \text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$$a_{n-1} a_n=\frac{a_{n-1}}{d}-\frac{a_n}{d} \ldots(n-1)$$

On adding Eqs. (i), (ii), ......, $$(n-1)$$, we get

$$\begin{array}{r} a_1 a_2+a_2 a_3+\ldots+a_{n-1} a_n \\ =\frac{a_1}{d}-\frac{a_n}{d} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Also, } \frac{1}{a_n}=\frac{1}{a_1}+(n-1) d \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{a_1-a_n}{d}=(n-1) a_1 a_n \\ & \therefore \quad a_1 a_2+a_2 a_3+\ldots+a_{n-1} a_n=(n-1) a_1 a_n \end{aligned}$$

en.content: The equation of the line passing through $$(-4,3,1)$$ parallel to the plane $$x+2 y-z-5=0$$ and intersecting the line $$\frac{x+1}{-3}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z-2}{-1}$$ is
en.explanation:
$$\text { Any point on the line } \frac{x+1}{-3}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z-2}{-1} \text {, is of the form } (-3 \lambda-1,2 \lambda+3,-\lambda+2).$$

Let the point $$A \equiv(-3 \lambda-1,2 \lambda+3,-\lambda+2)$$ and $$B \equiv(-4,3,1)$$

Then, $$\mathbf{A B}=(3 \lambda-3) \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \lambda \hat{\mathbf{j}}+(\lambda-1) \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

Now, as the line is parallel to the given plane, therefore $$\mathrm{AB}$$ will be parallel to the given plane and so $$\mathrm{AB}$$ will be perpendicular to the normal of plane.

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{AB} \cdot \mathrm{n}=0$$, where $$\mathrm{n}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ is normal to the plane.

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(3 \lambda-3) \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \lambda \hat{\mathbf{j}}+(\lambda-1) \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \lambda-3-4 \lambda-\lambda+1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-2 \lambda=2 \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 \end{aligned}$$

Now, the required equation is the equation of line joining $$A(2,1,3)$$ and $$B(-4,3,1)$$, which is

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{x-(-4)}{2-(-4)}=\frac{y-3}{1-3}=\frac{z-1}{3-1} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{x+4}{6}=\frac{y-3}{-2}=\frac{z-1}{2} \\ \text { or } & \frac{x+4}{3}=\frac{y-3}{-1}=\frac{z-1}{1} \end{array}$$
